exercises.title: REST vs GraphQL — choose the right API style
exercises.instructions:
For each scenario, choose REST or GraphQL and explain the key reason:
A: File upload for Flipkart product image
B: Swiggy app: restaurant list + menu + first 3 reviews in one call
C: Zerodha webhook: notify on order fill event
D: Razorpay merchant dashboard: web needs 20 fields, mobile needs 5 fields
E: Simple CRUD for internal config management tool
exercises.expected_solution:
A: File upload → REST
   GraphQL doesn't handle binary file uploads natively (multipart is hacky)
   REST: POST /products/{id}/images with multipart/form-data
   GraphQL workaround exists but complex — REST wins for file I/O

B: Nested data in one call → GRAPHQL
   One query: { restaurant(id) { name menu { items { name price } } reviews(first:3) { rating } } }
   REST would need 3 calls: /restaurants/{id}, /restaurants/{id}/menu, /restaurants/{id}/reviews
   GraphQL eliminates N round trips for nested data

C: Webhook notifications → REST
   Webhooks are server-to-server HTTP callbacks (REST)
   GraphQL subscriptions work for client-initiated real-time (browser ← server)
   Webhooks: server pushes to a client-registered URL → REST pattern

D: Different field sets per client → GRAPHQL
   Web query: { merchant { id name volume settlements disputes ... 20 fields } }
   Mobile query: { merchant { id name volume } }  ← same endpoint, fewer fields
   REST: would need /merchants/{id}/summary and /merchants/{id}/detail (or versioning)
   GraphQL: same schema, clients request what they need

E: Simple internal CRUD → REST
   Config management: GET/POST/PUT/DELETE on /configs
   No complex relationships, no multiple client types
   REST is simpler, better tooling for CRUD, team knows it
   GraphQL's benefits don't justify learning curve for simple CRUD

test_cases.expected:
A: File upload → REST
   GraphQL doesn't handle binary file uploads natively (multipart is hacky)
   REST: POST /products/{id}/images with multipart/form-data
   GraphQL workaround exists but complex — REST wins for file I/O

B: Nested data in one call → GRAPHQL
   One query: { restaurant(id) { name menu { items { name price } } reviews(first:3) { rating } } }
   REST would need 3 calls: /restaurants/{id}, /restaurants/{id}/menu, /restaurants/{id}/reviews
   GraphQL eliminates N round trips for nested data

C: Webhook notifications → REST
   Webhooks are server-to-server HTTP callbacks (REST)
   GraphQL subscriptions work for client-initiated real-time (browser ← server)
   Webhooks: server pushes to a client-registered URL → REST pattern

D: Different field sets per client → GRAPHQL
   Web query: { merchant { id name volume settlements disputes ... 20 fields } }
   Mobile query: { merchant { id name volume } }  ← same endpoint, fewer fields
   REST: would need /merchants/{id}/summary and /merchants/{id}/detail (or versioning)
   GraphQL: same schema, clients request what they need

E: Simple internal CRUD → REST
   Config management: GET/POST/PUT/DELETE on /configs
   No complex relationships, no multiple client types
   REST is simpler, better tooling for CRUD, team knows it
   GraphQL's benefits don't justify learning curve for simple CRUD

hints: File upload: REST wins (multipart/form-data)
Nested data: GraphQL eliminates multiple round trips
Simple CRUD: REST is simpler

exercises.title: Complete the GraphQL schema
exercises.instructions: Fill in the blanks to complete the Razorpay payment GraphQL schema.

blanks.hint: defines a set of allowed string values
defines types used as mutation arguments
query argument for fetching by id

hints: type: output objects; input: mutation args; enum: fixed value set
ID!: non-null opaque identifier
input parameter name: 'input' by convention

exercises.title: Write a Flipkart product GraphQL schema
exercises.instructions:
Write a complete GraphQL schema for Flipkart products:
1. Product: id, name, price (nested Price type), category (enum), description (nullable)
2. Review: id, rating (Int 1-5), comment, authorName
3. Category enum: ELECTRONICS, CLOTHING, BOOKS, HOME_KITCHEN, SPORTS
4. Query: product(id), products(category), searchProducts(keyword)
5. Mutation: createProduct(input), addReview(productId, input)
hints: Price as nested type: better than priceAmount on Product (extensible)
nullable return from query: product(id): Product (no !) — returns null if not found
input type for mutations: CreateProductInput not Product

exercises.title: Write GraphQL queries for the Flipkart schema
exercises.instructions:
Given the Flipkart schema from ex_3, write 4 GraphQL queries:
1. Fetch single product: id, name, price.amount, first-3 reviews' rating+comment
2. Fetch all ELECTRONICS products: id, name, price.amount, inStock
3. Search 'laptop': id, name, price.amount, price.discountedAmount, category
4. Create product mutation with all required fields
5. Validate: identify which fields are nullable in each result
hints: Query: request ONLY fields needed for this view (list vs detail)
Mutation: always fetch id and key fields after creating (to update client state)
Variables: never hardcode values in queries (use $variables)

exercises.title: GraphQL error handling — what can go wrong
exercises.instructions:
Write examples of GraphQL errors and how to handle them:
1. Field not found error (queried field doesn't exist in schema)
2. Null value for non-null field (resolver returned null for String!)
3. Query variable type mismatch (Float! argument receives String)
4. Partial success: data + errors in same response
5. @SpringBootTest: validate error response format
hints: Partial success: GraphQL unique — data + errors can coexist
Non-null null propagation: if String! resolves to null → parent becomes null
Error path: ["product", "reviews"] tells client exactly which field failed

exercises.title: Schema design for Zerodha portfolio
exercises.instructions:
Design Zerodha portfolio GraphQL schema:
1. Portfolio type: totalValue, positions (list of Position)
2. Position: symbol, quantity, averagePrice, currentPrice, pnl (calculated)
3. Trade history: TradeHistoryEntry with symbol, quantity, price, type (BUY/SELL), date
4. Query: portfolio(accountId), tradeHistory(accountId, limit)
5. Mutation: placeTrade(input: PlaceTradeInput)
6. Validate schema compiles without errors
hints: Default argument: limit: Int = 10 (client can omit, schema provides default)
Nullable price for PlaceTradeInput: market orders don't need a price
TradeResult vs Position: mutation returns its own type (not Position)

exercises.title: Introspection queries
exercises.instructions:
Write GraphQL introspection queries:
1. List all types in the schema
2. Get all fields on the Product type
3. Get all queries available
4. Check if a field is deprecated
5. @SpringBootTest: document introspection concept
hints: __schema: root introspection field (always available)
__type(name): inspect specific type
Disable introspection in production: security best practice

exercises.title: Schema evolution — backwards compatible changes
exercises.instructions:
Document and test schema evolution strategies:
1. Adding a new field: backwards compatible?
2. Removing a field: breaking change?
3. @deprecated directive usage
4. Renaming a field: breaking change?
5. Adding a non-null argument: breaking change?
6. @SpringBootTest: validate deprecation annotations
hints: Add nullable: safe; remove any field: breaking
Making optional arg required: breaking; adding optional arg: safe
@deprecated: mark field for removal, give clients migration path

exercises.title: GraphQL fragments for reusable field sets
exercises.instructions:
Write GraphQL fragments for the Swiggy menu schema:
1. Fragment: MenuItemFields (id, name, price, vegetarian, available)
2. Fragment: RestaurantBasicInfo (id, name, rating, deliveryTime)
3. Use fragments in 2 different queries
4. Named fragments vs inline fragments
5. @SpringBootTest: fragment concept documented
hints: ...FragmentName: spread fragment into a query
Inline fragment: ... on TypeName { fields } — for union/interface types
Fragment colocation: each UI component defines its own fragment (React/Apollo pattern)

exercises.title: GraphQL query execution analysis
exercises.instructions:
Given this schema and query, predict what data is fetched and what is NOT fetched:

Schema: Product { id, name, price { amount, currency }, category, description, reviews { id, rating, comment, author { name, email } }, seller { id, name, rating } }

Query:
```graphql
query { product(id: "P1") { name price { amount } reviews { rating } } }
```

1. Which fields are fetched from the database?
2. Which resolvers will be called?
3. What is NOT fetched (preventing over-fetching)?
4. What is the HTTP response structure?
exercises.expected_solution:
FIELDS FETCHED (database/resolvers called):
  product → fetches Product with id='P1'
    name → from product row (same DB call)
    price → Price resolver called (maybe same row or separate call)
      amount → from price object (currency NOT fetched!)
    reviews → Reviews resolver called (SELECT * FROM reviews WHERE product_id='P1')
      rating → from each review row (id, comment, author NOT fetched)

RESOLVERS CALLED:
  Query.product(id='P1') → main product lookup
  Product.price → resolves Price nested object
  Product.reviews → resolves review list
  (Review.author → NOT called: 'author' not in query)

NOT FETCHED (zero over-fetching):
  product.category → not in query → resolver NOT called
  product.description → not in query → resolver NOT called
  price.currency → not in query (only 'amount' requested)
  review.id, review.comment → not in query
  Review.author → entire author resolver SKIPPED
  Seller → entire seller resolver SKIPPED

HTTP RESPONSE STRUCTURE:
  POST /graphql
  { "data": {
      "product": {
        "name": "iPhone 15 Pro",
        "price": { "amount": 134900.0 },
        "reviews": [
          { "rating": 5 },
          { "rating": 4 },
          { "rating": 5 }
        ]
      }
  }}
  Note: Response mirrors EXACTLY the query shape
  Note: NO 'id', 'category', 'currency', 'comment', 'seller' in response

test_cases.expected:
FIELDS FETCHED (database/resolvers called):
  product → fetches Product with id='P1'
    name → from product row (same DB call)
    price → Price resolver called (maybe same row or separate call)
      amount → from price object (currency NOT fetched!)
    reviews → Reviews resolver called (SELECT * FROM reviews WHERE product_id='P1')
      rating → from each review row (id, comment, author NOT fetched)

RESOLVERS CALLED:
  Query.product(id='P1') → main product lookup
  Product.price → resolves Price nested object
  Product.reviews → resolves review list
  (Review.author → NOT called: 'author' not in query)

NOT FETCHED (zero over-fetching):
  product.category → not in query → resolver NOT called
  product.description → not in query → resolver NOT called
  price.currency → not in query (only 'amount' requested)
  review.id, review.comment → not in query
  Review.author → entire author resolver SKIPPED
  Seller → entire seller resolver SKIPPED

HTTP RESPONSE STRUCTURE:
  POST /graphql
  { "data": {
      "product": {
        "name": "iPhone 15 Pro",
        "price": { "amount": 134900.0 },
        "reviews": [
          { "rating": 5 },
          { "rating": 4 },
          { "rating": 5 }
        ]
      }
  }}
  Note: Response mirrors EXACTLY the query shape
  Note: NO 'id', 'category', 'currency', 'comment', 'seller' in response

hints: Response mirrors query shape exactly
Unrequested fields: resolvers NOT called (key performance benefit)
Author resolver: SKIPPED — author not in query despite being in schema

exercises.title: Complete Razorpay payment GraphQL schema + queries
exercises.instructions:
Build complete Razorpay payment GraphQL schema:
1. Payment, Merchant, Settlement, Dispute types with relationships
2. PaymentStatus and DisputeStatus enums
3. Input types: CreatePaymentInput, InitiateRefundInput
4. Query: payment, payments(merchantId, status, limit), merchant, disputes(merchantId)
5. Mutation: createPayment, initiateRefund, resolveManagedDisput
6. Write 3 client queries demonstrating different use cases
hints: payments on Merchant type: resolver fetches payments for that merchant (field-level argument)
dispute: nullable on Payment (payment may not have a dispute)
refunds: list on Payment (could have multiple partial refunds)

exercises.title: Custom scalar types
exercises.instructions:
Design and document custom GraphQL scalars:
1. DateTime scalar: ISO 8601 format
2. Rupee scalar: Long (amount in paise for precision)
3. PAN scalar: Indian PAN number format validation
4. Explain: when to use custom scalars vs String
5. @SpringBootTest: scalar type design documented
hints: Rupee as Long (paise): avoids Float precision bugs in financial calculations
Custom scalar: serialization (Java→JSON) + coercion (JSON→Java) + literal parsing
graphql-java-extended-scalars: DateTime, Email, URL, JSON scalars pre-built

exercises.title: Interface and Union types
exercises.instructions:
Design GraphQL interfaces and unions for a Swiggy search:
1. SearchResult union: Restaurant | MenuItem | DishCategory
2. Node interface: id field (common pattern)
3. Query: search(keyword) returns [SearchResult!]!
4. Client query using inline fragments
5. @SpringBootTest: union/interface design documented
hints: __typename: always available, tells client which concrete type was returned
Interface: implement when types share common fields
Union: for truly different types grouped as one return type

exercises.title: Complete Flipkart product catalog schema
exercises.instructions:
Build the complete Flipkart product GraphQL schema:
1. Product with variants (size/color), price history, seller info
2. Category hierarchy: Category (parent, children list)
3. Search filters as input type
4. Pagination: products(page, size) → ProductPage { items, total, hasNext }
5. 4 client queries for different pages: home, category, search, product detail
exercises.starter_code: # Complete Flipkart Product Catalog GraphQL Schema

expected_stdout_contains: Complete Flipkart Product Catalog GraphQL Schema

hints: ProductPage for pagination: items + total + hasNext (cursor-based is alternative)
Category.parent: nullable — top-level categories have no parent
ProductFilter input: all fields nullable (client provides only the filters they want)

exercises.title: Complete M27 T1 GraphQL Basics synthesis
exercises.instructions:
Build complete M27 T1 synthesis:
1. Write a Swiggy restaurant GraphQL schema (types, enums, inputs, queries, mutations)
2. Write 3 client queries (home page, restaurant page, search)
3. Document 3 safe vs 3 breaking schema changes
4. Write a fragment for reusable RestaurantCard fields
5. @SpringBootTest (3 tests): schema concepts, query structure, evolution rules
exercises.starter_code: // Complete M27 T1 GraphQL Basics synthesis
hints: Schema as String: stores SDL for documentation/testing
Fragment: defined once, spread with '...' in multiple queries
contains() checks: validate schema has required sections
